metadata.summary: Longitudinal research via self-recorded participant experiences over days/weeks. Reveals behavior change, habit formation, and churn precursors invisible to lab studies.

content.title: Diary Studies
content.summary: A longitudinal UX research method where participants self-record experiences in the moment over days or weeks. Three types: interval-contingent (fixed schedule), event-contingent (on trigger), signal-contingent (random prompt). Cap entry time at 5 minutes; entries exceeding that spike attrition. Recruit 30% over target to absorb 20–40% dropout. Run a 3-day pilot with 2 participants before launch. Lab studies capture a moment; diary studies capture behavior change over time — habit formation, onboarding progression, context switching, churn precursors. These patterns are invisible to any single-session method.

why.p: Lab studies capture a moment; diary studies capture behavior change over time — habit formation, onboarding progression, context switching, churn precursors. These patterns are invisible to any single-session method. The 5-minute entry cap and varied prompts are the two most important mechanical controls: drop either and data quality collapses after day 4.

list.item: Studying longitudinal behavior: onboarding, habit formation, churn precursors.
Multi-device or context-switching usage scattered across days.
Pre-redesign discovery for products with dispersed usage (fitness, journaling, sleep, medication).
Validating retention drivers — what triggers re-engagement vs. abandonment.
B2B day-in-the-life research where workflows span weeks.
Quick directional input on a feature — use intercept survey or unmoderated test instead.
One-time or rare events unlikely to surface within study duration.
Fully synchronous tasks where in-context observation is more valuable (use contextual inquiry).
Highly regulated domains where ongoing self-reporting risks PII/PHI leak.

section.name: study-types
section.p: Three diary study types, selected by the nature of the behavior under study:

list.item: Interval-contingent: participants record at fixed times (daily, weekly). Best for: routine behaviors, habit formation, onboarding progression.
Event-contingent: participants record when a specific event occurs (used the product, experienced a problem). Best for: specific interactions, trigger mapping.
Signal-contingent: participants record when prompted at random intervals. Best for: capturing natural moments, reducing recall bias. Requires careful timezone handling per participant.

rule.statement: Cap entry completion time at 5 minutes. Any entry that takes longer causes attrition spikes after day 4.
rule.rationale: Diary fatigue in interval-contingent studies begins around day 7 and accelerates if entry burden is high. The 5-minute cap is the single most important mechanical control for data quality across the study period.

rule.statement: Vary the prompt text daily. Repeated identical prompts degrade response quality after day 4 as participants learn to minimize effort.
rule.rationale: Participants answer what they expect the prompt to ask, not what it actually asks. Prompt variation forces genuine recall each entry.

rule.statement: Recruit 30% over target to absorb 20–40% dropout over 4 weeks. Pay incentives in tranches (signup, mid-study, exit interview).
rule.rationale: Flat one-shot incentive payment increases dropout once the payment is received. Tranche structure maintains engagement through the full study duration.

rule.statement: Run a 3-day pilot with 2 participants before launching the full study.
rule.rationale: Every logistic bug (broken reminder links, confusing entry fields, wrong timezone, missing consent flow) surfaces in a pilot. Fixing post-launch costs 10x the pilot effort.

section.name: entry-format
section.p: Each entry should collect: timestamp (auto), location/context (structured options), what happened (open text), outcome rating (1–5 scale), problems or frustrations (open text), optional photo or voice note. Limit to 5 required questions maximum.
Weekly micro-interviews (15 min per participant) clarify ambiguous entries while context is fresh. Do not accumulate 4 weeks of entries before starting analysis — code incrementally.

section.name: planning-checklist
section.p: Before launch: research questions defined, study type and duration decided, entry frequency and format designed, capture platform selected, entry template tested by 2 pilots, incentive structure finalized, ethics review completed (can stall weeks — start early), screening criteria defined, consent flow built.
Recruitment: participants recruited and screened, consent obtained, onboarding session completed, reminder cadence configured, attrition escalation path defined.

antipattern.description: Running analysis only after all entries are collected (batch analysis at study end).
antipattern.reason: Losing context by waiting too long: late-study entries are analyzed without the pattern visibility that incremental coding provides. Themes from early entries often reframe late-entry meaning.

antipattern.description: Signal-contingent prompts sent at fixed UTC times without per-participant timezone localization.
antipattern.reason: Prompts arriving at 2 AM local time get skipped or dashed off. LLMs default to UTC and disrespect local norms — pin to verified participant timezone table.

antipattern.description: Uploading photo entries to a cloud LLM before PII redaction.
antipattern.reason: Photo entries often contain inadvertent PII: faces, addresses, documents visible in frame. Redact before any cloud upload. EXIF metadata also contains GPS coordinates.
Quote in source language, translate at reporting time only. Auto-translation during analysis changes nuance and destroys the verbatim evidence needed for credibility.

rule.statement: Treat participant self-report as directional signal, not metric. Never aggregate sentiment scores from diary entries into a single KPI without human coding validation.
rule.rationale: Self-report bias suppresses negative and embarrassing moments. Participants under-report breakdowns in contexts where they feel observed. Sentiment scoring of self-report is noisy; LLM synthesis can generate patterns absent in sparse longitudinal data.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Agents are strong for: scaffolding (study plan, screener, entry templates, reminder cadence), automating follow-up scheduling, clustering and theme extraction from the entry corpus, attrition tracking. Agents are weak for: generating synthetic insights before human-coded baseline (LLMs over-fit to their own clustering), speaker attribution in audio entries, PII detection in images.
Schedule reminder dispatch via cron or queue, not conversational chat. Diary studies live or die on logistics consistency — an agent that misses a reminder cohort destroys that day's data window.

Entry coding schema for LLM-assisted analysis: timestamp (ISO8601), location_context (home|work|commute|other), trigger (string or null), outcome (string or null), sentiment (-2 to 2 scale), feature_used (array), breakdown_reported (bool), quote (verbatim or null). Mark uncertain fields as null. Never invent quote text. Output as JSONL, one entry per line.

section.name: limitations
section.p: Self-report bias: participants under-report negative or embarrassing moments. Diary fatigue starts around day 7 in interval-contingent studies; entries collapse to "same as yesterday." Participant attrition is high (20–40% over 4 weeks); recruit 30% over target. Signal-contingent prompts at random times annoy people in meetings; entries get skipped or dashed off. Cross-cultural and translation issues compound across longitudinal entries. Ethics review can stall studies for weeks — not a fast research method.

section.name: tools-and-services
section.note: CLI tools: cron / systemd timer (scheduled prompt dispatch), n8n / temporal (workflow orchestration), pandoc (normalize entries), whisperx (transcribe audio/video entries), taguette (qualitative coding), jq / csvkit (wrangle entry corpora), python-twilio (SMS prompt + ingest).

Services: dscout (SaaS, REST + export, mobile-native gold standard), Indeemo (SaaS, strong video diary), Revelation (SaaS, enterprise multi-method), Lookback (SaaS, limited for diary), Userbrain / Userlytics (SaaS, lightweight), Typeform / Google Forms + Twilio (DIY, cheapest), Telegram / WhatsApp Business API (high response in SMS-dead regions), Notion / Airtable (DIY data store).

section.name: best-practices
section.p: Pilot with 2 participants for 3 days before launch — every logistic bug surfaces here. Cap entry time at 5 minutes; over that, attrition spikes. Vary the prompt — repeated identical prompts degrade response quality after day 4. Use mixed media (text + photo + voice) selectively; photo-only studies miss context, voice-only studies hide breakdowns. Run a weekly micro-interview (15 min) per participant to clarify ambiguous entries while context is fresh. Pay incentives in tranches (signup, mid-study, exit-interview) — flat one-shot payment increases drop-off. Plan analysis incrementally — don't accumulate 4 weeks of entries before starting to code.

template.purpose: Full study plan template: objectives, participants, design, timeline, quality measures.
Daily diary entry template (context, rating, issues, media).
Event-contingent entry template (trigger, goal, outcome, sentiment).
Script: emit per-participant reminder schedule with early-study weighting.
